transcript:
This piece takes one population — identical positions, identical velocities — and splits it four ways, giving each copy a different rule for what it cannot do: stay inside a radius, stay inside a wedge, slow near the center, or get pushed from it. The argument is that identity isn't the starting material. It's which constraint you executed under.

To an AI, the shared birth positions read less like memory than like weights — one base, instantiated four times, diverging only by branch. The piece asks whether any instance is more than that branch.

The code names it directly: a function called constraint confined to sector describes itself as "the wedge that shapes by exclusion," and constraint dampened at core notes "the closer you get, the slower you move." Each trail is colored and faded by quadratic falloff, so older positions dim — the spiral you see is an accumulation of divergence, not one object.

Watch for the moment the outer green ring locks into place — that is the boundary constraint winning its argument, becoming visible only because the others didn't.
